title: The Boss Over The Board
content:
آ آ آ آ When heï؟½s over a rough and unpopular shed,
آ آ آ آ With the sins of the bank and the men on his head;
آ آ آ آ When he musnï؟½t look black or indulge in a grin,
آ آ آ آ And thirty or forty men hate him like Sin,
آ آ آ آ I am moved to admit, when the total is scored,
آ آ آ آ That itï؟½s just a bit off for the Boss-of -the-board.
آ آ آ آ I have battled a lot,
آ آ آ آ But my dreamï؟½s never soared
آ آ آ آ To the lonely position of Boss-of-the-board.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas a black-listed shed down the Darling: the Boss
آ آ آ آ Was a small man to see, though a big man to cross,
آ آ آ آ We had nought to complain of, except what we thought,
آ آ آ آ And the Boss didnï؟½t boss any more than he ought;
آ آ آ آ But the Union was booming, and Brotherhood soared,
آ آ آ آ So we hated like poison the Boss-of-the-board.
آ آ آ آ We could tolerate ï؟½handsï؟½,
آ آ آ آ We respected the cook;
آ آ آ آ But the name of a Boss was a blot in our book.

آ آ آ آ Heï؟½d a row with Big Duggan, a rough sort of Jim,
آ آ آ آ Or, rather, Jim Duggan was ï؟½laying forï؟½ him!
آ آ آ آ His hate of Injustice and Greed was so deep
آ آ آ آ That his shearing grew rough, and he ill-used the sheep.
آ آ آ آ And I fancied that Duggan his manliness lowerï؟½d
آ آ آ آ When he took off his shirt to the Boss-of-the-board,
آ آ آ آ For the Boss was ten stone,
آ آ آ آ And the shearer full-grown,
آ آ آ آ And he might have, they said, let the crawler alone.

آ آ آ آ Though some of us there wished the fight to the strong,
آ آ آ آ Yet we knew in our hearts that the shearer was wrong.
آ آ آ آ And the crawler was plucky, it canï؟½t be denied,
آ آ آ آ For he had to fight Freedom and Justice beside,
آ آ آ آ But he came up so gamely, as often as floored,
آ آ آ آ That a blackleg stood up for the Boss-of-the-board!
آ آ آ آ And the fight was a sight,
آ آ آ آ And we pondered that night,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Itï؟½s surprising how some of those blacklegs can fight!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Next day at the office, when sadly the wreck
آ آ آ آ Of Jim Duggan came up like a lamb for his cheque,
آ آ آ آ Said the Boss, ï؟½Donï؟½t be childish! Itï؟½s all past and gone;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½I am short of good shearers. Youï؟½d better stay on.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And we fancied Jim Duggan our dignity lowerï؟½d
آ آ آ آ When he stopped to oblige a damned Boss-of-the-board.
آ آ آ آ We said nothing to Jim,
آ آ آ آ For a joke might be grim,
آ آ آ آ And the subject, we saw, was distasteful to him.

آ آ آ آ The Boss just went on as heï؟½d done from the first,
آ آ آ آ And he favoured Big Duggan no more than the worst;
آ آ آ آ And when weï؟½d cut out and the steamer came down,
آ آ آ آ With the hawkers and spielers, to take us to town,
آ آ آ آ And weï؟½d all got aboard, ï؟½twas Jim Duggan, good Lord!
آ آ آ آ Who yelled for three cheers for the Boss-of-the-board.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas a bit off, no doubt,
آ آ آ آ And with Freedom about,
آ آ آ آ But a lot is forgot when a shed is cut out.

آ آ آ آ With Freedom of Contract maintained in his shed,
آ آ آ آ And the curse of the Children of Light on his head,
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s apt to long sadly for sweetheart or wife,
آ آ آ آ And his views be inclined to the dark side of life.
آ آ آ آ The Truth must be spread and the Cause must be shored,
آ آ آ آ But itï؟½s just a bit rough on the Boss-of-the-board.
آ آ آ آ I am all for the Right,
آ آ آ آ But perhaps (out of sight)
آ آ آ آ As a son or a husband or father heï؟½s white.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
